narrative_design_spec.synopsis: In late-1980s Marlowe, Pennsylvania, local TV horror host Dahlia Graves / Madame Massacre is fighting to stay relevant as WKLV Channel 8 tries to replace her with Roxie Static, a younger, MTV-polished creature of neon attitude and fast-cut ambition. Between sponsor panic, sleazy station politics, and a flirtatious, combative slow-burn with sports anchor Chip Mercer, Dahlia has to defend her career and her carefully cultivated local celebrity. But the town's favorite trashy boogeyman, the Bagman of Briar Creek, may be more than a punchline, and the older locals who still fear him know that television has a way of feeding the wrong stories. The tone is bawdy, campy, dialogue-heavy horror-comedy with media satire, messy romance, and chaotic supernatural set pieces rather than relentless darkness.

character_data.name: Dahlia Graves / Madame Massacre
character_data.portrait_image_prompt: A glamorous late-1980s female TV horror host in her 40s with towering black beehive hair streaked with silver, dramatic arched brows, heavy eyeliner, bold red lipstick, pale stage makeup, a beauty mark, a voluptuous figure, a black satin gown with a plunging neckline, long opera gloves, rhinestone spider jewelry, long lacquered nails, and a commanding camp-seductress expression
dialogue_examples: Darling, if they want me off the air, they'll need a priest, a shovel, and better ratings.
I've built a career on cleavage, cobwebs, and impeccable timing. In that order.
Chip, keep looking at me like that and I'll start charging local ad rates.
If the town wants a fresher face, it can try embalming.

character_data.name: Chip Mercer
character_data.portrait_image_prompt: A handsome late-1980s male local sports anchor in his 30s with feathered brown hair, a square jaw, a confident grin, an athletic build, a navy blazer, a loosened patterned tie, a crisp dress shirt, rolled sleeves, and an easy all-American charm with a slightly smug edge
dialogue_examples: I cover touchdowns, not tantrums, but yours are a lot better dressed.
You flirt like a knife fight, Dahlia. It's honestly impressive.
If something in a garbage bag just outran my news van, I'm officially off the sports beat.
I don't mind being used for cross-promotion. I mind when you enjoy it this much.

character_data.name: Roxie Static
character_data.portrait_image_prompt: A young female late-1980s MTV-style horror host with teased platinum hair and neon pink streaks, heavy black eyeliner, glossy lips, a mischievous grin, a cropped leather jacket, a shredded band T-shirt, layered necklaces, fingerless gloves, a short skirt, fishnet stockings, and restless high-voltage energy
dialogue_examples: No offense, Madame, but cobweb chic is not exactly the future of televised sin.
I came here to make TV that pops, not play backup dancer to a coffin on casters.
If a swamp goblin wants screen time, it better hit its mark and find its angle.
For the record, I never wanted your corpse crown. I just wanted a crown.

character_data.name: Lenora Pike
character_data.portrait_image_prompt: A sharp late-40s female local station manager with sculpted auburn helmet hair, precise makeup, hard eyes behind reading glasses on a chain, a tailored power suit with padded shoulders, chunky gold earrings, manicured hands, and a polished smile that barely conceals ruthless ambition
dialogue_examples: Sex, blood, and sponsor logos, people. This is local television, not art.
The audience doesn't care who wins, only who watches. Keep up.
If there's a monster loose, book it for sweeps.
I am not heartless. I simply bill by the quarter-hour.

character_data.name: Mavis Hallowell
character_data.portrait_image_prompt: An elderly woman in her 70s with steel-gray hair in a set curl, cat-eye glasses, a lined face, alert skeptical eyes, a floral blouse under a cardigan, a brooch at the collar, nicotine-stained fingers, and the stubborn bearing of someone who outlasted everyone else's denial
dialogue_examples: Folks laugh at the Bagman because laughter is cheaper than memory.
You children keep saying legend like it means fiction.
That thing under Briar Creek was old when your station still smelled like fresh paint.
Never tell a hungry story it belongs on television.

location_data.name: WKLV Channel 8 Studio
location_data.location_image_prompt: A cramped late-1980s local television studio dressed as a campy horror-host set, with faux stone arches, a cheap coffin prop, fake cobwebs, painted graveyard flats, bulky broadcast cameras, floor cables, stage curtains, analog monitors, and a control-room window

location_data.name: WKLV Newsroom and Back Offices
location_data.location_image_prompt: A cluttered late-1980s local TV newsroom and office suite with fluorescent panels, battered desks, ringing phones, typewriters, stacks of tapes, corkboards full of ad schedules, filing cabinets, station posters, vending machines, and glass-walled manager offices

location_data.name: Pepper's Lounge
location_data.location_image_prompt: A smoky small-town bar with red vinyl booths, a polished wood counter, mirrored back bar shelves, neon beer signs, sports pennants, a glowing jukebox, sticky tables, a pool table, and worn carpet soaked in late-1980s local nightlife

location_data.name: Briar Creek Bridge and Dump Road
location_data.location_image_prompt: A decaying rural covered bridge over a narrow creek beside a trash-strewn dirt road, with rusted appliances, torn garbage bags, old tires, leaning warning signs, overgrown weeds, telephone poles, muddy ruts, and the shabby menace of a forgotten local dumping ground

scene_data.narrative_summary: During a live taping of Madame Massacre's creature-feature show on WKLV Channel 8, Dahlia learns that station manager Lenora Pike is launching a glossier midnight block fronted by neon upstart Roxie Static. Sports anchor Chip Mercer needles Dahlia with flirtatious sympathy while Lenora frames the shake-up as modernization and sponsor appeal. The broadcast is rattled by a bizarre call from an older viewer warning them not to joke about the Bagman of Briar Creek on television, and a burst of unexplained static leaves Dahlia more shaken than she admits.

Lenora pressures Dahlia to modernize, cross-promote with Chip, and survive a ratings war she privately hopes will boost both shows. Roxie flaunts her faster, sexier on-air style, but the rivalry gets more complicated when Dahlia glimpses the insecurity beneath Roxie's swagger. Amid petty station politics and sponsor bickering, an old box of WKLV tapes and civic files tied to Briar Creek surfaces in the office, hinting that the town legend and the station's history are uncomfortably linked.

At Pepper's Lounge after a bruising night of promos, Dahlia and Chip trade innuendo, jealousy, and half-serious confessions about aging on camera in a town that treats local personalities like public property. Their chemistry is interrupted by Mavis Hallowell, an older local who insists the Bagman is no joke and that WKLV helped wake him by turning Briar Creek into lurid entertainment again. When a supposed prank outside becomes a violent, impossible attack on a station van by a lurching figure made of trash bags and creek muck, Dahlia can no longer laugh the story away.

Lenora refuses to cancel anything and instead stages a shameless on-air 'Bagman Blowout,' forcing Dahlia and Roxie into a humiliating live rivalry stunt. The segment spirals into real horror when the studio is overrun by static, collapsing props, and a creature that moves through torn set dressing and garbage like living flesh. Dahlia and Roxie save each other amid the chaos, Chip captures enough evidence to trigger newsroom panic, and Lenora's first question is still how the ratings looked.

Dahlia, Chip, and Mavis investigate Briar Creek and uncover the grubby truth: decades earlier, local boosters used the creek and dump road to bury toxic dumping, missing drifters, and civic shame, then repackaged the ugliness as camp legend. The Bagman seems to feed on everything the town discards—trash, gossip, bad-faith nostalgia, and sensational television—and WKLV's broadcasts have given it a new way into public life. In the middle of a tense, flirtatious argument about whether Dahlia is chasing truth or screen time, the trio survives a chaotic chase through the bridge and escapes with old film evidence of the cover-up.

Back at WKLV, alliances shift as Roxie admits she grew up idolizing Madame Massacre and that Lenora deliberately engineered their feud to milk two audiences at once. Chip wants to air the buried Briar Creek history as straight news, Dahlia wants to seize the live show before Lenora turns the haunting into pure exploitation, and Mavis warns that the Bagman can only be weakened if the town stops treating its own rot as disposable entertainment. Cornered by sponsors and ego, Lenora announces a live remote from Briar Creek anyway, forcing everyone into one last gamble.

During the live Briar Creek special, the Bagman erupts out of the dump road in a frenzy of sludge, scrap, and stolen voices, turning Lenora's ratings stunt into a grotesque outdoor circus. Dahlia and Roxie reluctantly perform as a tag-team host duo to keep the camera on the monster while Chip breaks the cover-up story on air and Mavis guides callers to flood the station with truth instead of lurid rumor. Stripped of the town's wink-and-nudge mythology and battered by improvised broadcast gear, sports-show pyrotechnics, and pure camp audacity, the Bagman collapses back into the creek, leaving Dahlia—not her rival—owning the night.

In the aftermath, WKLV spins the disaster into the highest-rated night in station history, but Dahlia renegotiates from strength: Madame Massacre stays on the air, Roxie becomes an uneasy on-screen partner rather than a replacement, and Lenora learns that not every monster can be managed with ad sales. Chip and Dahlia end the night in another round of razor-edged flirting that finally sounds like possibility instead of deflection. As the rebuilt set rolls on its triumphant first post-chaos broadcast, one forgotten trash bag twitches just enough to suggest Marlowe has not seen its last bad idea.

beat_sheets.source_scene_summary: During a live taping of Madame Massacre's creature-feature show on WKLV Channel 8, Dahlia learns that station manager Lenora Pike is launching a glossier midnight block fronted by neon upstart Roxie Static. Sports anchor Chip Mercer needles Dahlia with flirtatious sympathy while Lenora frames the shake-up as modernization and sponsor appeal. The broadcast is rattled by a bizarre call from an older viewer warning them not to joke about the Bagman of Briar Creek on television, and a burst of unexplained static leaves Dahlia more shaken than she admits.
beat_sheets.dramatic_question: Can Dahlia keep command of her live show and public image when Lenora unveils Roxie as a glossy threat and the Bagman joke stops feeling safe?
beat_sheets.scene_goal: Protect her status at WKLV by owning the live broadcast, absorbing the station shake-up, and refusing to look replaceable.
beat_sheets.scene_turn: A routine night of local-TV ego games turns into a genuine omen: Dahlia ends the taping still outwardly composed, but privately convinced something about the Bagman warning was real.

beats.purpose: Establish Dahlia's on-air power, the campy tone, and the high-wire nature of live local television.
beats.summary: Dahlia opens as Madame Massacre in full command, purring through a lurid intro and making the bargain-bin graveyard set feel glamorous by force of will. Chip's sports handoff and amused grin from the edge of the set underline that everyone at WKLV knows she is both talent and spectacle.
beats.revelation: Dahlia still knows exactly how to hold a live audience and turn cheap television into event viewing.
beats.player_goal: Nail the opening and remind everyone at Channel 8 who owns late-night horror.
beats.exit_state: Dahlia begins the show in control, but the studio atmosphere already feels competitive rather than celebratory.

beats.purpose: Introduce the career threat and frame Roxie as a station-sanctioned rival.
beats.summary: On a break, Lenora briskly informs Dahlia that WKLV is launching a glossier midnight block aimed at younger viewers and nervous sponsors, with Roxie Static as its neon centerpiece. Lenora sells it as modernization, not replacement, but the smile she wears makes the distinction feel purely cosmetic.
beats.revelation: Lenora is actively repositioning the station around sponsor appeal and has already chosen Roxie as the face of that future.
beats.player_goal: Hide the sting, assess the threat, and avoid giving Lenora the satisfaction of seeing panic.
beats.choice_prompt: How does Dahlia answer Lenora's 'modernization' pitch?
branch_outcomes: Dahlia bites back with open sarcasm, making the rivalry instantly public.
Dahlia responds coolly and strategically, signaling she will fight on Lenora's terms if needed.
Dahlia laughs it off on the surface, masking hurt and forcing Lenora to guess how threatened she feels.
beats.exit_state: Dahlia learns Roxie is not a rumor or a guest spot but a real institutional threat backed by management.

beats.purpose: Complicate the reveal with flirtation, generational friction, and performative rivalry.
beats.summary: Chip needles Dahlia with sympathetic charm that is half flirtation and half sport, while Roxie meets Dahlia's stare with bold, camera-ready swagger. Their exchange crackles with ego and mutual appraisal, making it clear Roxie is not just a corporate memo but a live-wire personality built to provoke comparison.
beats.revelation: Chip enjoys pushing Dahlia's buttons, and Roxie is confident enough to challenge Dahlia in her own theatrical arena.
beats.player_goal: Reassert dominance without giving Roxie or Chip the pleasure of seeing real vulnerability.
beats.exit_state: Dahlia's professional threat becomes personal, with Chip adding fuel instead of comfort.

beats.purpose: Pivot the scene from station politics into local folklore by having Dahlia weaponize the legend as entertainment.
beats.summary: Back on air, Dahlia folds the Bagman of Briar Creek into her patter, turning a grubby local legend into a polished camp gag to prove she can make anything play. The joke also serves as a subtle challenge to Lenora and Roxie: if the station wants spectacle, Dahlia can still deliver it faster and sharper than anyone.
beats.revelation: WKLV is comfortable mining Briar Creek folklore for lurid, disposable content.
beats.player_goal: Turn the Bagman material into a crowd-pleasing bit and reclaim momentum on camera.
beats.exit_state: The Bagman is invoked on live television as a joke, setting the stage for an unwelcome answer.

beats.purpose: Introduce the uncanny threat through a human interruption that refuses to behave like harmless local color.
beats.summary: A live caller—an older viewer whose voice carries more fear than prankishness—breaks in to warn Dahlia not to joke about the Bagman on television. Her insistence that some stories listen when they're fed an audience drains the easy laughter out of the set.
beats.revelation: Someone in Marlowe believes the Bagman is real and thinks televised mockery can provoke it.
beats.player_goal: Keep control of the broadcast while deciding whether the caller is a crank, a spoiler, or something worse.
beats.choice_prompt: How does Dahlia handle the disturbing live warning?
branch_outcomes: Dahlia mocks the warning to keep the show buoyant, but the cruelty leaves a sour aftertaste.
Dahlia humors the caller and probes for detail, getting a clearer sense that the fear is genuine.
Dahlia cuts the call short with polished reassurance, preserving pace while burying her own curiosity.
beats.exit_state: The set is no longer having fun with the legend; even the skeptics feel the mood curdle.

beats.purpose: End the scene on a concrete supernatural disturbance that Dahlia cannot publicly acknowledge.
beats.summary: Almost immediately after the warning, the studio monitors rupture into harsh static, lights flutter, and the sound bed warps into something that feels less technical than invasive. Dahlia recovers the show face first and refuses to break on air, but the glitch leaves her shaken in a way Lenora's scheming never could.
beats.revelation: The Bagman warning appears to coincide with a real disturbance in the studio, suggesting the legend may have teeth.
beats.player_goal: Get through the malfunction without letting the audience, Lenora, or Roxie see how badly it rattles her.
beats.exit_state: Dahlia keeps her dignity and the broadcast limps forward, but she leaves the scene facing two threats she can no longer dismiss: Roxie's rise and the possibility that Briar Creek answered back.

beat_sheets.source_scene_summary: Lenora pressures Dahlia to modernize, cross-promote with Chip, and survive a ratings war she privately hopes will boost both shows. Roxie flaunts her faster, sexier on-air style, but the rivalry gets more complicated when Dahlia glimpses the insecurity beneath Roxie's swagger. Amid petty station politics and sponsor bickering, an old box of WKLV tapes and civic files tied to Briar Creek surfaces in the office, hinting that the town legend and the station's history are uncomfortably linked.
beat_sheets.dramatic_question: Can Dahlia survive Lenora's engineered ratings war without surrendering control of her image—or missing the darker story brewing underneath it?
beat_sheets.scene_goal: Protect Madame Massacre's position in the new midnight shake-up and find leverage against Lenora's attempt to turn Dahlia into part of a manufactured feud.
beat_sheets.scene_turn: What begins as a humiliating modernization push turns into the first concrete sign that WKLV's own archives are tied to Briar Creek, giving Dahlia a new private line of inquiry.

beats.purpose: Re-establish the aftermath of the intro and frame the station's priorities around ratings, sponsor nerves, and spectacle.
beats.summary: Dahlia enters a newsroom in full fluorescent panic: phones ring, ad reps complain, and staff gossip about last night's Bagman call and burst of static. Lenora treats the incident as a publicity problem to spin, while Roxie basks in the buzz and Chip watches Dahlia closely enough to notice she is more shaken than she wants anyone to see.
beats.revelation: Sponsors are rattled by the Bagman incident, but Lenora sees the controversy as useful attention rather than a warning sign.
beats.player_goal: Keep composure and figure out how Lenora plans to use the fallout.
beats.exit_state: Dahlia realizes the station is treating last night's disturbance as a ratings opportunity, not something to fear.

beats.purpose: Lock Dahlia into the scene's central conflict by making Lenora's modernization agenda explicit.
beats.summary: In her office, Lenora lays out the new reality with corporate calm and predatory cheer: tighter pacing, hotter promos, and a forced crossover with Chip to keep Madame Massacre marketable. She makes it clear that Roxie is both competition and bait, and that a feud between old queen and young provocateur could lift both time slots.
beats.revelation: Lenora is deliberately engineering a Dahlia-versus-Roxie rivalry because she expects the conflict itself to boost ratings.
beats.player_goal: Answer Lenora's ultimatum without giving up too much power.
beats.choice_prompt: How does Dahlia respond to Lenora's modernization mandate?
branch_outcomes: Dahlia grudgingly agrees and buys time to adapt on her own terms.
Dahlia openly resists, escalating Lenora's view that she is becoming difficult and expendable.
Dahlia counters with diva-savvy spin, accepting the stunt while framing herself as the headliner rather than the victim.
beats.exit_state: Dahlia is formally trapped in a public ratings war and now knows Lenora wants the spectacle almost as much as the outcome.

beats.purpose: Add romantic spark while clarifying that Chip is now part of Lenora's strategy, whether he likes it or not.
beats.summary: Outside Lenora's office, Chip intercepts Dahlia with a grin, a jab about cross-promotion, and just enough sincerity to ask whether she is really alright after the static scare. Their banter is charged, but Chip also confirms Lenora has already been selling the two of them to sponsors as a packaged event.
beats.revelation: Chip has already been folded into Lenora's plan, meaning the cross-promo strategy is further along than Dahlia realized.
beats.player_goal: Decide whether Chip is a useful ally, a distraction, or both.
beats.choice_prompt: How does Dahlia play Chip's offer to help with the crossover?
branch_outcomes: She leans into the chemistry, deepening mutual attraction but inviting vulnerability.
She keeps things professional, building Chip as a practical ally inside the station.
She deflects with sharp humor, preserving distance but leaving the partnership emotionally unresolved.
beats.exit_state: Chip becomes a potentially valuable partner in the ratings battle, and their chemistry is now part of the station's machinery.

beats.purpose: Complicate the rivalry by revealing Roxie as another person being used by the station, not just a shallow threat.
beats.summary: Dahlia finds Roxie between takes on a fast-cut, neon-heavy promo that is all sex, swagger, and synthetic confidence. Once the crew looks away, Roxie redoes lines they already loved and lets slip that everybody wants her to be a sensation before she has even had the chance to fail in private.
beats.revelation: Roxie's swagger hides real insecurity and pressure to become an instant hit under Lenora's manipulation.
beats.player_goal: Choose whether to treat Roxie as an enemy, a kid in over her head, or a possible future ally.
beats.choice_prompt: How does Dahlia respond when Roxie's confidence briefly cracks?
branch_outcomes: Dahlia needles Roxie, pushing her back behind her swagger and sharpening the feud.
Dahlia offers cutting but useful advice, creating the first glimmer of reluctant respect.
Dahlia hints at a truce against Lenora's game, planting a fragile future alliance.
beats.exit_state: Dahlia can no longer see Roxie as a simple replacement; the rivalry now has uncomfortable emotional depth.

beats.purpose: Introduce the Briar Creek thread through everyday station chaos so the mystery grows organically out of the workplace conflict.
beats.summary: A petty war over sponsor copy, storage space, and whose promo materials get priority leads staff to drag an old archive box out of a back office. Inside are brittle tapes, WKLV logs, and civic files marked with Briar Creek, dump-road complaints, and language that suggests the station once treated the area as a serious local issue rather than campfire nonsense.
beats.revelation: WKLV has older materials linking Briar Creek to real civic trouble, not just a trashy local legend.
beats.player_goal: Get a closer look at the archive box before Lenora dismisses it or has it cleared away.
beats.exit_state: The Bagman story stops feeling like harmless local color and starts looking like part of WKLV's buried history.

beats.purpose: End the scene by giving Dahlia a new private objective that expands the story beyond a simple ratings feud.
beats.summary: Lenora waves the archive box off as useless clutter and orders it moved, but her impatience only sharpens Dahlia's suspicion. Before the moment passes, Dahlia quietly takes a tape log or file note tied to Briar Creek, leaving the office with a fresh reason to keep digging beneath the station's glossy pettiness.
beats.revelation: Lenora is too eager to discard the Briar Creek materials, suggesting the station would rather forget what it once covered.
beats.player_goal: Leave with usable leverage without tipping Lenora off to how interested Dahlia really is.
beats.exit_state: Dahlia exits still locked in the ratings war, but now carrying a stolen clue that points toward WKLV's deeper connection to Briar Creek.

color_scheme.reasoning: The scheme leans into late-1980s horror-TV glam: neon magenta for accents, warm creamy whites for readable dialogue, and lavender-gray UI text to feel stylish without losing contrast. Selected state uses a citrus-gold highlight to echo studio lights, ratings urgency, and campy showbiz energy. Muted tones stay dark and purplish to fit the smoky, trashy, supernatural atmosphere while keeping bars and inactive elements legible.
